question: A 68-year-old man has resting 'pill-rolling' hand movements, slowed movements, and a shuffling gait progressing over years. Which pathological finding best explains his hypokinetic disorder?
cited_learning_objective: Understand the pathogenesis of Parkinson’s disease and “atypical” Parkinson’s disease

options.text: Loss of dopaminergic neurons in substantia nigra
options.rationale: Correct: Parkinsonian hypokinesia arises from loss of dopaminergic SNc neurons reducing striatal dopamine and increasing inhibitory basal ganglia output, as described in the lecture.

options.text: Degeneration of cerebellar Purkinje cells causing ataxia
options.rationale: Wrong: Cerebellar Purkinje cell loss causes ataxia and coordination deficits; it would not produce the classic parkinsonian hypokinetic syndrome described.

options.text: Anterior horn cell loss producing flaccid weakness
options.rationale: Wrong: Anterior horn cell loss causes lower motor neuron signs (flaccidity, fasciculations); changing this to corticospinal tract loss would be needed to mimic spastic features.

options.text: Oligodendroglial cytoplasmic inclusions with demyelination
options.rationale: Wrong: Oligodendroglial α-synuclein inclusions characterize multiple system atrophy and may cause parkinsonism plus autonomic features; absence of SNc neuron loss would make this option correct instead.

options.text: Striatal neuronal intranuclear ubiquitinated inclusions
options.rationale: Wrong: Intranuclear ubiquitinated inclusions are typical of polyglutamine diseases like Huntington disease causing chorea; if SNc neurons were preserved, this would fit Huntington pathology.

question: Which clinical timing distinguishes Parkinson disease with associated dementia from dementia with Lewy bodies in a patient with both motor and cognitive symptoms?
cited_learning_objective: Understand the pathogenesis of Parkinson’s disease and “atypical” Parkinson’s disease

options.text: Cognitive symptoms occur more than one year later
options.rationale: Correct: The lecture states Parkinson’s disease-associated dementia is when cognitive symptoms begin more than one year after motor onset.

options.text: Cognitive symptoms occur within one week of motor onset
options.rationale: Wrong: Cognitive symptoms within one year (not one week) of motor onset indicate dementia with Lewy bodies; changing the interval to under one year would make this correct.

options.text: Motor symptoms resolve before cognitive decline begins
options.rationale: Wrong: Motor symptom resolution is not characteristic; if motor symptoms improved and cognitive decline persisted, this would not match either diagnosis.

options.text: Cognitive symptoms precede motor symptoms by years
options.rationale: Wrong: Cognitive-first presentation can occur but the lecture specifically uses the one-year interval to distinguish PD dementia versus Lewy body dementia; earlier cognitive onset alone is insufficient.

options.text: Motor and cognitive symptoms begin simultaneously always
options.rationale: Wrong: Simultaneous onset is not required and is inconsistent with the lecture distinction; if simultaneous onset occurred within one year it would favor Lewy body dementia.

question: A neuropathology slide shows eosinophilic intraneuronal inclusions rich in a presynaptic protein. Which aggregate is this most consistent with?
cited_learning_objective: Understand the pathogenesis of Parkinson’s disease and “atypical” Parkinson’s disease

options.text: Alpha-synuclein containing Lewy bodies in neurons
options.rationale: Correct: The lecture identifies Lewy bodies as eosinophilic neuronal inclusions rich in α-synuclein.

options.text: Tau containing neurofibrillary tangles in neurons
options.rationale: Wrong: Tau tangles are tau protein aggregates seen in tauopathies like progressive supranuclear palsy; if immunostain showed tau rather than α-synuclein this would be correct.

options.text: Ubiquitinated huntingtin intranuclear inclusions
options.rationale: Wrong: Huntingtin inclusions are intranuclear and ubiquitinated in Huntington disease; a nuclear location and CAG expansion history would be required to make this correct.

options.text: TDP-43 cytoplasmic inclusions in motor neurons
options.rationale: Wrong: TDP-43 inclusions are linked to ALS and frontotemporal dementia; presence in motor neurons with UMN/LMN signs would support this instead.

options.text: Prion protein amyloid plaques diffusely in cortex
options.rationale: Wrong: Prion disease shows PrP amyloid and spongiform change; cortical spongiosis and rapidly progressive dementia would be needed to make this correct.

question: Which environmental exposures mentioned in the lecture can produce parkinsonian syndromes by damaging dopaminergic neurons?
cited_learning_objective: Provide examples of acquired metabolic and toxic agent-induced diseases relevant to disease in the CNS

options.text: Certain pesticides and contaminants in synthetic heroin
options.rationale: Correct: The lecture lists pesticides and chemicals in synthetic heroin as toxins that may induce parkinsonism by damaging dopaminergic neurons.

options.text: Excess dietary copper from shellfish consumption
options.rationale: Wrong: Wilson disease involves copper metabolism but dietary shellfish excess is not cited; demonstration of ATP7B mutation and hepatic copper accumulation would be needed.

options.text: Thiamine deficiency from alcohol causing mammillary damage
options.rationale: Wrong: Thiamine deficiency causes Wernicke encephalopathy with mammillary body lesions and ataxia, not primary parkinsonism; evidence of nutritional deficiency would change this.

options.text: GAA trinucleotide expansions in frataxin gene
options.rationale: Wrong: GAA expansions cause Friedreich’s ataxia with mitochondrial dysfunction and ataxia, not toxin-induced parkinsonism; genetic testing showing GAA repeats would be required.

options.text: SMN1 loss of function causing motor neuron loss
options.rationale: Wrong: SMN1 mutations cause spinal muscular atrophy with LMN loss; genetic confirmation of SMN1 deletion would be necessary to make this correct.

question: A patient with early-onset parkinsonism has poor response to levodopa and prominent autonomic failure with orthostatic hypotension. Which atypical syndrome from the lecture best fits this presentation?
cited_learning_objective: Understand the pathogenesis of Parkinson’s disease and “atypical” Parkinson’s disease

options.text: Multiple system atrophy with oligodendroglial inclusions
options.rationale: Correct: MSA often presents with parkinsonism plus autonomic dysfunction and oligodendroglial α-synuclein inclusions, and is minimally levodopa responsive per the lecture.

options.text: Progressive supranuclear palsy with vertical gaze palsy
options.rationale: Wrong: PSP features supranuclear gaze palsy and early falls rather than prominent autonomic failure; presence of vertical gaze impairment would favor PSP.

options.text: Corticobasal degeneration with cortical apraxia
options.rationale: Wrong: Corticobasal degeneration presents with asymmetric cortical signs and apraxia; cortical dysfunction and limb apraxia would be required to support this diagnosis.

options.text: Parkinson disease with early dementia and hallucinations
options.rationale: Wrong: PD with dementia may have hallucinations but prominent autonomic failure and poor levodopa response point away from idiopathic PD; earlier motor response to levodopa would change this.

options.text: Wilson disease with hepatic copper accumulation
options.rationale: Wrong: Wilson disease can cause movement disorders and autonomic features but typically has hepatic signs and Kayser-Fleischer rings; abnormal copper studies would be needed to make this correct.

question: Which molecular dysfunctions are implicated in Parkinson disease pathogenesis beyond protein aggregation according to the lecture?
cited_learning_objective: Understand the pathogenesis of Parkinson’s disease and “atypical” Parkinson’s disease

options.text: Mitochondrial, lysosomal, and endosomal dysfunction
options.rationale: Correct: The lecture lists abnormal mitochondrial, lysosomal, and endosomal function as contributors to PD pathogenesis.

options.text: Loss of frataxin causing mitochondrial iron overload
options.rationale: Wrong: Frataxin loss causes Friedreich’s ataxia with mitochondrial iron dysregulation; demonstration of GAA expansions in frataxin would be needed to make this correct.

options.text: SMN1 spliceosome assembly failure in motor neurons
options.rationale: Wrong: SMN1 defects underlie spinal muscular atrophy via spliceosome assembly failure, not PD; genetic SMN1 loss would be required to support this.

options.text: Excess extracellular glutamate causing excitotoxicity
options.rationale: Wrong: Glutamate excitotoxicity is implicated in ALS pathogenesis per the lecture; evidence of UMN/LMN signs and motor neuron loss would favor ALS.

options.text: Copper accumulation in basal ganglia causing toxicity
options.rationale: Wrong: Copper accumulation is central to Wilson disease; abnormal copper studies and hepatic involvement would be necessary to make this correct.

question: A 42-year-old man has progressive choreiform movements, personality changes, and a family history of similar illness. Genetic testing shows 44 CAG repeats in the implicated gene. Which statement best describes the genetic finding?
cited_learning_objective: Explain the genetics of Huntington disease

options.text: CAG repeat expansion with definite disease at this length
options.rationale: Correct: The lecture states 40+ CAG repeats confer definite Huntington disease; 44 repeats fall in this range.

options.text: Intermediate unstable allele not causing disease now
options.rationale: Wrong: 29–35 repeats are intermediate; 44 repeats exceed that and are disease causing, so this option is incorrect unless repeat count were 29–35.

options.text: Juvenile onset expected with over fifty five repeats
options.rationale: Wrong: Juvenile-onset is associated with ≥55 repeats; 44 repeats do not predict juvenile onset, so this statement is not applicable.

options.text: Incomplete penetrance likely with this repeat number
options.rationale: Wrong: 36–39 repeats show incomplete penetrance; 44 repeats are fully penetrant, so this option would be correct only if repeats were 36–39.

options.text: CAG repeat length correlates with symptom severity
options.rationale: Wrong: The lecture notes repeat length correlates with age of onset but not severity of initial symptoms; severity correlation would require different evidence.

question: Which gross brain change is most characteristic of Huntington disease on autopsy?
cited_learning_objective: Explain the genetics of Huntington disease

options.text: Marked caudate nucleus atrophy with ventricular enlargement
options.rationale: Correct: The lecture describes striking caudate atrophy with dilation of lateral ventricles as characteristic of Huntington disease.

options.text: Substantia nigra pallor with loss of pigment
options.rationale: Wrong: Substantia nigra pallor is typical of Parkinson disease; if SNc neuron loss and Lewy bodies were present this would be correct.

options.text: Cerebellar vermis shrinkage with folia loss
options.rationale: Wrong: Cerebellar atrophy is more typical of spinocerebellar ataxias; cerebellar degeneration and gait ataxia would be required to support this.

options.text: Diffuse cortical spongiform change and vacuolation
options.rationale: Wrong: Spongiform change suggests prion disease; rapidly progressive dementia and PrP pathology would be needed to make this correct.

options.text: Anterior horn cell loss with spinal cord atrophy
options.rationale: Wrong: Anterior horn cell loss is seen in motor neuron diseases like SMA or poliomyelitis; LMN signs and spinal cord pathology would be necessary to justify this.

question: A family shows earlier age at onset of Huntington disease across generations, especially when transmitted by fathers. Which genetic phenomenon from the lecture explains this observation?
cited_learning_objective: Explain the genetics of Huntington disease

options.text: Anticipation due to expansion during spermatogenesis
options.rationale: Correct: The lecture notes CAG repeats tend to expand in the male germline, producing genetic anticipation with earlier onset in successive generations.

options.text: Mitochondrial inheritance from affected mothers
options.rationale: Wrong: Mitochondrial inheritance is maternal and unrelated to CAG repeat expansion; maternal transmission would not explain paternal expansion effects.

options.text: X-linked recessive transmission causing male bias
options.rationale: Wrong: Huntington disease is autosomal dominant, not X-linked; an X-linked pattern would require a gene on the X chromosome.

options.text: Anticipation due to trinucleotide contraction in eggs
options.rationale: Wrong: Anticipation in HD is due to expansion, not contraction, and is more pronounced with paternal transmission; contraction in oocytes would not explain earlier onset.

options.text: Somatic mosaicism causing variable penetrance
options.rationale: Wrong: Somatic mosaicism can affect phenotype but the lecture emphasizes germline expansion during spermatogenesis as the cause of anticipation.

question: Which histologic feature in remaining striatal neurons is described in Huntington disease pathology?
cited_learning_objective: Explain the genetics of Huntington disease

options.text: Intranuclear inclusions of ubiquitinated huntingtin protein
options.rationale: Correct: The lecture states remaining striatal and cortical neurons have intranuclear ubiquitinated huntingtin aggregates.

options.text: Lewy bodies composed of alpha-synuclein
options.rationale: Wrong: Lewy bodies are α-synuclein inclusions seen in PD; finding α-synuclein rather than huntingtin would change the diagnosis.

options.text: Tau positive neurofibrillary tangles in cortex
options.rationale: Wrong: Tau tangles are characteristic of tauopathies like PSP; tau immunoreactivity would be required to support that pathology.

options.text: TDP-43 cytoplasmic inclusions in motor neurons
options.rationale: Wrong: TDP-43 inclusions are linked to ALS/FTD; motor neuron degeneration and UMN/LMN signs would be needed to make this correct.

options.text: Prion protein plaques with spongiform change
options.rationale: Wrong: Prion disease shows PrP plaques and spongiosis; rapidly progressive dementia and PrP positivity would be necessary to justify this option.

question: A teenager presents with progressive gait ataxia, scoliosis, and hypertrophic cardiomyopathy. Genetic testing reveals GAA repeat expansions in both alleles of a mitochondrial-associated gene. Which diagnosis from the lecture is most likely?
cited_learning_objective: Explain the relationship of Friedreich’s ataxia to other spinocerebellar ataxias

options.text: Friedreich’s ataxia due to frataxin GAA expansions
options.rationale: Correct: The lecture describes Friedreich’s ataxia as autosomal recessive with GAA expansions in frataxin causing mitochondrial dysfunction, ataxia, scoliosis, and cardiomyopathy.

options.text: Spinocerebellar ataxia type with CAG expansions
options.rationale: Wrong: Many SCAs are autosomal dominant with CAG polyglutamine expansions; a dominant inheritance pattern and CAG repeats would be required to make this correct.

options.text: Vitamin B12 deficiency causing subacute combined degeneration
options.rationale: Wrong: B12 deficiency causes dorsal column and corticospinal tract degeneration with sensory loss, not hypertrophic cardiomyopathy; abnormal B12 labs would be needed to support this.

options.text: Multiple system atrophy with olivopontocerebellar involvement
options.rationale: Wrong: MSA can cause olivopontocerebellar degeneration and ataxia but typically presents in adulthood and is not linked to frataxin GAA expansions; oligodendroglial inclusions would support MSA.

options.text: Wilson disease with hepatic copper deposition
options.rationale: Wrong: Wilson disease can cause neurologic signs and movement disorders but is associated with copper metabolism defects and hepatic disease; abnormal copper studies would be necessary.

question: Which cellular role of frataxin explains the vulnerability of heart and nervous tissue in Friedreich’s ataxia?
cited_learning_objective: Explain the relationship of Friedreich’s ataxia to other spinocerebellar ataxias

options.text: Mitochondrial iron regulation and prevention of oxidative damage
options.rationale: Correct: The lecture states frataxin regulates mitochondrial iron levels; loss leads to mitochondrial dysfunction and oxidative damage affecting high-energy tissues like heart and nerve.

options.text: Nuclear DNA repair during replication stress
options.rationale: Wrong: Nuclear DNA repair is not the primary frataxin function described; evidence of DNA repair defects would be needed to support this option.

options.text: Synaptic vesicle release at presynaptic terminals
options.rationale: Wrong: Synaptic vesicle function is associated with proteins like α-synuclein; presynaptic dysfunction would not explain cardiomyopathy seen in Friedreich’s ataxia.

options.text: Assembly of the spliceosome for mRNA processing
options.rationale: Wrong: SMN1 is involved in spliceosome assembly in SMA; demonstration of SMN1 loss would be required to make this correct.

options.text: Copper transport across hepatocyte membranes
options.rationale: Wrong: Copper transport defects underlie Wilson disease via ATP7B; abnormal copper studies and hepatic pathology would be necessary to justify this.

question: A patient with progressive mixed upper and lower motor neuron signs develops respiratory failure within five years. Which disease and typical pathological tract loss from the lecture best match this course?
cited_learning_objective: Summarize the clinical features, pathogenesis, and morphological characteristics of amyotrophic lateral sclerosis

options.text: Amyotrophic lateral sclerosis with corticospinal tract degeneration
options.rationale: Correct: The lecture describes ALS as mixed UMN and LMN disease with loss of myelinated fibers in lateral corticospinal tracts leading to progressive weakness and respiratory failure.

options.text: Spinal muscular atrophy with anterior horn cell loss
options.rationale: Wrong: SMA causes LMN loss without UMN signs and typically presents in childhood; SMN1 loss and early onset would be required to make this correct.

options.text: Kennedy disease causing X-linked lower motor neuron signs
options.rationale: Wrong: Kennedy disease is X-linked and primarily LMN with endocrine features; absence of UMN signs and presence of gynecomastia would support this instead.

options.text: Multiple system atrophy with autonomic failure and ataxia
options.rationale: Wrong: MSA affects multiple systems including autonomic and cerebellar circuits but does not classically produce combined UMN and LMN degeneration leading to respiratory failure as in ALS.

options.text: Progressive supranuclear palsy with gaze palsy and rigidity
options.rationale: Wrong: PSP presents with supranuclear gaze palsy and axial rigidity rather than combined motor neuron degeneration; vertical gaze impairment would favor PSP.

question: Which pathogenic mechanisms are highlighted in the lecture as contributors to sporadic ALS?
cited_learning_objective: Summarize the clinical features, pathogenesis, and morphological characteristics of amyotrophic lateral sclerosis

options.text: Genetic factors, RNA processing proteins, and glutamate excitotoxicity
options.rationale: Correct: The lecture cites genetic factors, proteins involved in RNA processing, and glutamate-mediated excitotoxicity as implicated in ALS pathogenesis.

options.text: Alpha-synuclein aggregation in oligodendrocytes
options.rationale: Wrong: Oligodendroglial α-synuclein inclusions are characteristic of MSA; demonstration of oligodendroglial inclusions would be needed to support that diagnosis.

options.text: Frataxin deficiency causing mitochondrial iron overload
options.rationale: Wrong: Frataxin deficiency causes Friedreich’s ataxia; evidence of GAA expansions and cardiomyopathy would be required to make this correct.

options.text: CAG repeat expansions producing polyglutamine toxicity
options.rationale: Wrong: CAG expansions underlie Huntington disease and some SCAs; genetic testing showing expanded CAG repeats would be necessary to justify this option.

options.text: Thiamine deficiency causing mammillary body hemorrhage
options.rationale: Wrong: Thiamine deficiency causes Wernicke encephalopathy with mammillary body lesions, not ALS; nutritional deficiency evidence would be required to make this correct.

question: A spinal cord specimen shows attenuation of anterior motor roots with relative preservation of posterior sensory roots. Which disease from the lecture does this pattern most closely indicate?
cited_learning_objective: Summarize the clinical features, pathogenesis, and morphological characteristics of amyotrophic lateral sclerosis

options.text: Amyotrophic lateral sclerosis with anterior root degeneration
options.rationale: Correct: The lecture describes ALS pathology showing attenuation of anterior motor roots compared with posterior sensory roots due to motor neuron loss.

options.text: Subacute combined degeneration affecting dorsal columns
options.rationale: Wrong: Subacute combined degeneration from B12 deficiency affects dorsal columns and corticospinal tracts, not selective anterior root attenuation; abnormal B12 labs would change this.

options.text: Poliomyelitis causing anterior horn cell destruction
options.rationale: Wrong: Poliomyelitis can cause anterior horn cell loss and root attenuation but is infectious and acute; viral history and CSF findings would be needed to support poliomyelitis.

options.text: Peripheral neuropathy with demyelination of sensory fibers
options.rationale: Wrong: Peripheral demyelinating neuropathies often affect sensory fibers and posterior roots; electrophysiologic sensory deficits would be required to make this correct.

options.text: Spinal muscular atrophy with congenital anterior horn loss
options.rationale: Wrong: SMA causes anterior horn cell loss but typically presents in infancy or childhood; SMN1 mutation evidence and early onset would be necessary to justify this.

question: A middle-aged man with progressive asymmetric rigidity, frequent falls, and difficulty with voluntary vertical gaze has tau-containing inclusions on pathology. Which atypical parkinsonian syndrome does this represent?
cited_learning_objective: Understand the pathogenesis of Parkinson’s disease and “atypical” Parkinson’s disease

options.text: Progressive supranuclear palsy with tau pathology
options.rationale: Correct: The lecture describes PSP as presenting with truncal rigidity, falls, vertical gaze difficulty, and tau-containing neuronal and glial inclusions.

options.text: Corticobasal degeneration with cortical tau inclusions
options.rationale: Wrong: Corticobasal degeneration features asymmetric cortical signs and apraxia; prominent cortical apraxia and alien limb phenomena would favor corticobasal degeneration.

options.text: Multiple system atrophy with oligodendroglial alpha-synuclein
options.rationale: Wrong: MSA shows oligodendroglial α-synuclein inclusions and autonomic failure rather than supranuclear gaze palsy; autonomic dysfunction would support MSA instead.

options.text: Parkinson disease with Lewy body dementia overlap
options.rationale: Wrong: PD with Lewy bodies may have dementia and parkinsonism but vertical gaze palsy and tau inclusions point to PSP; presence of Lewy bodies rather than tau would change this.

options.text: Wilson disease with hepatic and basal ganglia copper
options.rationale: Wrong: Wilson disease involves copper deposition and hepatic signs; abnormal copper studies and liver involvement would be necessary to make this correct.

question: Which inheritance pattern and typical age of onset distinguish Friedreich’s ataxia from most spinocerebellar ataxias discussed in the lecture?
cited_learning_objective: Explain the relationship of Friedreich’s ataxia to other spinocerebellar ataxias

options.text: Autosomal recessive with onset in adolescence
options.rationale: Correct: The lecture emphasizes Friedreich’s ataxia is autosomal recessive and typically begins between ages 10 and 15, unlike most autosomal dominant SCAs.

options.text: Autosomal dominant with onset in late adulthood
options.rationale: Wrong: Most SCAs are autosomal dominant, but Friedreich’s ataxia is recessive; late adult onset would suggest other SCA types.

options.text: X-linked recessive with male predominance
options.rationale: Wrong: Friedreich’s ataxia is not X-linked; an X-linked pattern would require linkage to the X chromosome and male-biased transmission.

options.text: Mitochondrial inheritance with maternal transmission
options.rationale: Wrong: Friedreich’s ataxia is nuclear autosomal recessive due to frataxin GAA expansions; maternal-only transmission would indicate mitochondrial disease.

options.text: Autosomal dominant with juvenile onset always
options.rationale: Wrong: Juvenile onset can occur in some dominant SCAs but Friedreich’s ataxia is recessive and juvenile onset is typical but not 'always'; inheritance pattern would need to be recessive.

question: Which clinical triad best matches classic Parkinsonism as outlined in the lecture?
cited_learning_objective: Understand the pathogenesis of Parkinson’s disease and “atypical” Parkinson’s disease

options.text: Tremor, rigidity, and bradykinesia with instability
options.rationale: Correct: The lecture defines parkinsonism by tremor, rigidity, bradykinesia, and postural instability.

options.text: Ataxia, dysmetria, and intention tremor on action
options.rationale: Wrong: Cerebellar signs like ataxia and intention tremor characterize cerebellar disease and SCAs, not classic parkinsonism.

options.text: Fasciculations, flaccid weakness, and muscle atrophy
options.rationale: Wrong: These are LMN signs seen in motor neuron diseases like ALS or SMA; presence of UMN signs would alter the diagnosis.

options.text: Chorea, behavioral change, and caudate atrophy
options.rationale: Wrong: Chorea with caudate atrophy describes Huntington disease; genetic CAG expansion evidence would be needed to support that diagnosis.

options.text: Orthostatic hypotension, urinary incontinence, and impotence
options.rationale: Wrong: These autonomic features are prominent in MSA; prominent parkinsonism plus autonomic failure would favor MSA over idiopathic PD.

question: Which statement about early-onset Parkinson disease cases is consistent with the lecture?
cited_learning_objective: Understand the pathogenesis of Parkinson’s disease and “atypical” Parkinson’s disease

options.text: Often inherited and can be more aggressive clinically
options.rationale: Correct: The lecture notes early-onset PD cases are often inherited and may be aggressive, with under 10% having known genetic defects.

options.text: Always caused by pesticide exposure in childhood
options.rationale: Wrong: While toxins can induce parkinsonism, early-onset familial cases are genetic rather than always toxin-induced; toxin exposure evidence would be required.

options.text: Characterized by predominant cerebellar signs and ataxia
options.rationale: Wrong: Cerebellar signs indicate cerebellar disease or SCAs, not early-onset PD; cerebellar pathology would be needed to support this.

options.text: Typically reversible with dopamine antagonist withdrawal
options.rationale: Wrong: Drug-induced parkinsonism from dopamine antagonists can be reversible, but inherited early-onset PD is not reversible; drug history would change this.

options.text: Associated with juvenile Huntington disease in families
options.rationale: Wrong: Juvenile Huntington disease is due to very large CAG expansions and is genetically distinct from early-onset PD; CAG testing would be necessary to consider HD.

question: A patient with suspected Wilson disease presents with asymmetric tremor and psychiatric symptoms. Which feature mentioned in the lecture supports considering Wilson disease in the differential?
cited_learning_objective: Provide examples of acquired metabolic and toxic agent-induced diseases relevant to disease in the CNS

options.text: Neuropsychiatric features occur in ten to twenty percent
options.rationale: Correct: The lecture notes psychiatric features affect 10–20% of Wilson disease patients, supporting its inclusion in the differential for movement plus psychiatric signs.

options.text: Always presents with early severe dementia and hallucinations
options.rationale: Wrong: Wilson disease does not always present with severe dementia and hallucinations; hepatic and copper studies would be needed to confirm WD.

options.text: Is primarily caused by thiamine deficiency from alcoholism
options.rationale: Wrong: Thiamine deficiency causes Wernicke encephalopathy, not Wilson disease; nutritional history and thiamine levels would be required to support that diagnosis.

options.text: Is an X-linked disorder affecting only males
options.rationale: Wrong: Wilson disease is autosomal recessive, not X-linked; family history and genetic testing would clarify inheritance.

options.text: Is characterized by frataxin GAA repeat expansions
options.rationale: Wrong: Frataxin GAA expansions cause Friedreich’s ataxia; genetic testing for frataxin repeats would be necessary to support that diagnosis.

question: Which diagnostic neuropathologic gross finding differentiates Parkinson disease from normal control tissue as described in the lecture?
cited_learning_objective: Understand the pathogenesis of Parkinson’s disease and “atypical” Parkinson’s disease

options.text: Pallor of substantia nigra and locus ceruleus
options.rationale: Correct: The lecture lists pallor of the substantia nigra and locus ceruleus due to loss of neuromelanin-containing neurons as a gross PD finding.

options.text: Marked caudate enlargement with ventricular compression
options.rationale: Wrong: Caudate enlargement is not typical; Huntington disease shows caudate atrophy and ventricular dilation rather than enlargement causing compression.

options.text: Diffuse cortical atrophy with widened sulci
options.rationale: Wrong: Diffuse cortical atrophy is nonspecific and more typical of advanced dementia; focal SNc pallor distinguishes PD specifically.

options.text: Hypertrophic cardiomyopathy with myocardial fibrosis
options.rationale: Wrong: Hypertrophic cardiomyopathy is associated with Friedreich’s ataxia, not PD; cardiac findings and frataxin testing would be needed to support that diagnosis.

options.text: Spongiform change with numerous vacuoles in cortex
options.rationale: Wrong: Spongiform cortical change indicates prion disease; rapidly progressive dementia and PrP pathology would be required to make this correct.

question: Which clinical feature is most characteristic of corticobasal degeneration among the atypical parkinsonian syndromes described in the lecture?
cited_learning_objective: Understand the pathogenesis of Parkinson’s disease and “atypical” Parkinson’s disease

options.text: Asymmetric motor disturbances with cortical apraxia
options.rationale: Correct: The lecture describes corticobasal degeneration as having asymmetric motor disturbances and impaired higher cortical function such as apraxia.

options.text: Early autonomic failure with orthostatic hypotension
options.rationale: Wrong: Early autonomic failure is more typical of MSA; prominent autonomic dysfunction would point toward MSA rather than corticobasal degeneration.

options.text: Vertical gaze palsy with early falls and rigidity
options.rationale: Wrong: Vertical gaze palsy and early falls are classic for PSP; presence of gaze palsy would favor PSP over corticobasal degeneration.

options.text: Prominent chorea with caudate atrophy on imaging
options.rationale: Wrong: Chorea with caudate atrophy suggests Huntington disease; genetic CAG expansion testing would be needed to support that diagnosis.

options.text: Subacute combined degeneration with dorsal column loss
options.rationale: Wrong: Subacute combined degeneration from B12 deficiency affects dorsal columns and corticospinal tracts, not cortical apraxia and asymmetric rigidity.

question: A patient with progressive weakness shows both hyperreflexia and muscle fasciculations. Which combination of signs from the lecture indicates mixed upper and lower motor neuron disease?
cited_learning_objective: Summarize the clinical features, pathogenesis, and morphological characteristics of amyotrophic lateral sclerosis

options.text: Spastic weakness with fasciculations and muscle atrophy
options.rationale: Correct: The lecture describes ALS as mixed UMN (spasticity, hyperreflexia) and LMN (fasciculations, atrophy) leading to combined signs.

options.text: Flaccid paralysis with absent reflexes only
options.rationale: Wrong: Flaccid paralysis with absent reflexes indicates pure LMN lesion; presence of UMN signs would be required to indicate mixed disease.

options.text: Intention tremor with dysdiadochokinesia on exam
options.rationale: Wrong: Intention tremor and dysdiadochokinesia are cerebellar signs, not UMN/LMN mixed findings; cerebellar imaging would be needed to support that.

options.text: Resting pill-rolling tremor with rigidity
options.rationale: Wrong: Resting tremor and rigidity are parkinsonian features due to basal ganglia dysfunction, not mixed motor neuron disease; SNc pathology would be expected.

options.text: Sensory loss with stocking glove distribution
options.rationale: Wrong: Sensory peripheral neuropathy causes stocking-glove sensory loss and is not a feature of ALS; abnormal sensory testing would be necessary to support neuropathy.

question: Which therapeutic implication follows from the lecture statement that Parkinson disease involves loss of dopaminergic output to the striatum?
cited_learning_objective: Understand the pathogenesis of Parkinson’s disease and “atypical” Parkinson’s disease

options.text: Dopamine replacement improves motor symptoms initially
options.rationale: Correct: Because PD results from loss of dopaminergic SNc output to the striatum, dopamine replacement (e.g., L-DOPA) ameliorates motor symptoms early in disease as implied by the pathophysiology.

options.text: Cholinesterase inhibitors reverse substantia nigra neuron loss
options.rationale: Wrong: Cholinesterase inhibitors treat cognitive symptoms but do not restore SNc dopaminergic neurons; evidence of cholinergic deficit reversal would be required to make this correct.

options.text: Copper chelation will restore striatal dopamine levels
options.rationale: Wrong: Copper chelation treats Wilson disease, not idiopathic PD; abnormal copper studies and WD diagnosis would be necessary to justify chelation.

options.text: Frataxin gene therapy will prevent dopaminergic degeneration
options.rationale: Wrong: Frataxin therapy targets Friedreich’s ataxia mitochondrial dysfunction, not PD dopaminergic neuron loss; frataxin mutation evidence would be needed to consider this.

options.text: SMN1 replacement will improve basal ganglia function
options.rationale: Wrong: SMN1 replacement treats SMA by restoring spliceosome assembly in motor neurons, not basal ganglia dopaminergic deficits; SMN1 mutation presence would be required.

question: Which feature differentiates multiple system atrophy from Parkinson disease according to the lecture?
cited_learning_objective: Understand the pathogenesis of Parkinson’s disease and “atypical” Parkinson’s disease

options.text: Oligodendroglial alpha-synuclein inclusions with autonomic failure
options.rationale: Correct: The lecture highlights MSA pathology as α-synuclein inclusions in oligodendrocytes and prominent autonomic dysfunction distinguishing it from PD.

options.text: Neuronal Lewy bodies with early dementia and hallucinations
options.rationale: Wrong: Neuronal Lewy bodies are characteristic of PD and dementia with Lewy bodies; presence of neuronal Lewy bodies rather than oligodendroglial inclusions would indicate PD/LBD.

options.text: Tau inclusions with supranuclear gaze palsy
options.rationale: Wrong: Tau inclusions and gaze palsy are features of PSP; tau pathology and vertical gaze impairment would favor PSP.

options.text: Frataxin deficiency with cardiomyopathy and scoliosis
options.rationale: Wrong: Frataxin deficiency causes Friedreich’s ataxia with cardiomyopathy and scoliosis, not MSA; GAA repeat testing would be needed to support frataxin deficiency.

options.text: Anterior horn cell loss with fasciculations and atrophy
options.rationale: Wrong: Anterior horn cell loss is seen in motor neuron diseases like ALS or SMA; LMN signs and spinal pathology would be required to support that diagnosis.

question: A patient with suspected ALS has preserved sensation and bowel/bladder function but progressive dysarthria and tongue atrophy. Which pattern from the lecture supports ALS rather than a sensory neuropathy?
cited_learning_objective: Summarize the clinical features, pathogenesis, and morphological characteristics of amyotrophic lateral sclerosis

options.text: Mixed UMN and LMN signs with no sensory deficits
options.rationale: Correct: The lecture notes ALS presents with mixed upper and lower motor neuron signs and typically lacks sensory or bowel/bladder deficits, matching this pattern.

options.text: Predominant sensory loss with preserved motor strength
options.rationale: Wrong: Predominant sensory loss indicates peripheral neuropathy; abnormal sensory testing would be needed to support neuropathy instead of ALS.

options.text: Isolated autonomic failure with orthostatic hypotension
options.rationale: Wrong: Isolated autonomic failure suggests MSA or autonomic neuropathy; autonomic testing and orthostatic changes would be required to justify that diagnosis.

options.text: Cerebellar signs with intention tremor and dysmetria
options.rationale: Wrong: Cerebellar signs point to cerebellar disease or SCAs; presence of ataxia and dysmetria would favor cerebellar pathology.

options.text: Rapidly progressive dementia with cortical myoclonus
options.rationale: Wrong: Rapid dementia with myoclonus suggests prion disease; EEG and PrP studies would be necessary to support that diagnosis.

question: Which genetic disorder discussed in the lecture is X-linked and presents with lower motor neuron degeneration plus endocrine features like gynecomastia?
cited_learning_objective: Summarize the clinical features, pathogenesis, and morphological characteristics of amyotrophic lateral sclerosis

options.text: Kennedy disease due to androgen receptor trinucleotide expansion
options.rationale: Correct: The lecture describes Kennedy disease as X-linked with androgen receptor trinucleotide expansion causing LMN degeneration and androgen insensitivity features like gynecomastia.

options.text: Spinal muscular atrophy from SMN1 loss of function
options.rationale: Wrong: SMA is autosomal recessive due to SMN1 loss and lacks endocrine gynecomastia; SMN1 mutation evidence would be needed to support SMA.

options.text: Huntington disease with chorea and cognitive decline
options.rationale: Wrong: Huntington disease is autosomal dominant with chorea and cognitive decline due to CAG expansions, not X-linked endocrine features.

options.text: Wilson disease with hepatic copper accumulation
options.rationale: Wrong: Wilson disease is autosomal recessive with copper accumulation causing hepatic and neuropsychiatric signs, not X-linked androgen receptor defects.

options.text: Friedreich’s ataxia with cardiomyopathy and scoliosis
options.rationale: Wrong: Friedreich’s ataxia is autosomal recessive with frataxin GAA expansions causing cardiomyopathy and scoliosis, not X-linked endocrine features.

question: Which diagnostic laboratory or imaging finding would most directly support a diagnosis of Wilson disease in a patient with movement disorder and psychiatric symptoms, per the lecture context?
cited_learning_objective: Provide examples of acquired metabolic and toxic agent-induced diseases relevant to disease in the CNS

options.text: Low serum ceruloplasmin with elevated hepatic copper content
options.rationale: Correct: The lecture notes Wilson disease is a copper metabolism disorder; low ceruloplasmin and hepatic copper accumulation are diagnostic laboratory findings supporting WD.

options.text: High serum alpha-fetoprotein with hepatic mass lesion
options.rationale: Wrong: Elevated AFP suggests hepatocellular carcinoma, not Wilson disease; imaging and tumor markers would be needed to support that diagnosis.

options.text: GAA repeat expansions in frataxin gene on genetic testing
options.rationale: Wrong: GAA expansions indicate Friedreich’s ataxia; genetic testing showing GAA repeats would be required to diagnose FA rather than WD.

options.text: CAG repeat expansion in huntingtin gene on testing
options.rationale: Wrong: CAG expansion in huntingtin confirms Huntington disease; such a finding would point away from Wilson disease.

options.text: Low vitamin B12 with megaloblastic anemia on CBC
options.rationale: Wrong: Low B12 causes subacute combined degeneration and megaloblastic anemia, not Wilson disease; B12 deficiency labs would be necessary to support that diagnosis.

question: Which lecture-described feature best explains why Friedreich’s ataxia preferentially affects neurons and cardiac muscle?
cited_learning_objective: Explain the relationship of Friedreich’s ataxia to other spinocerebellar ataxias

options.text: High energy demand tissues suffer from mitochondrial dysfunction
options.rationale: Correct: The lecture explains mutated frataxin causes mitochondrial dysfunction and oxidative damage, affecting tissues with high mitochondrial energy demands like neurons and heart.

options.text: Selective loss of dopaminergic neurons in substantia nigra
options.rationale: Wrong: Selective SNc dopaminergic loss underlies PD, not Friedreich’s ataxia; SNc pathology would be required to support PD.

options.text: Autoimmune attack on peripheral myelin sheaths
options.rationale: Wrong: Autoimmune demyelination affects peripheral nerves (e.g., GBS) and is not the mechanism in FA; immune markers and demyelination evidence would be needed.

options.text: Excess copper deposition causing oxidative injury
options.rationale: Wrong: Copper deposition causes Wilson disease; abnormal copper studies and hepatic involvement would be necessary to justify this option.

options.text: Polyglutamine toxicity from CAG repeat expansions
options.rationale: Wrong: Polyglutamine toxicity from CAG repeats causes Huntington disease and some SCAs, not FA which involves GAA repeats in frataxin; CAG expansion evidence would be required.

question: Which clinical outcome is most commonly responsible for death in patients with Parkinson disease as noted in the lecture?
cited_learning_objective: Understand the pathogenesis of Parkinson’s disease and “atypical” Parkinson’s disease

options.text: Aspiration pneumonia or traumatic falls from instability
options.rationale: Correct: The lecture states death in PD is often from aspiration pneumonia or traumatic falls due to postural instability.

options.text: Acute myocardial infarction from coronary atherosclerosis
options.rationale: Wrong: While MI can cause death, the lecture specifically cites aspiration pneumonia and falls as common causes in PD; cardiac disease evidence would be needed to support MI as the cause.

options.text: Fulminant hepatic failure from Wilson disease
options.rationale: Wrong: Fulminant hepatic failure is a complication of Wilson disease, not idiopathic PD; abnormal liver tests and copper studies would be required to support this.

options.text: Respiratory failure from motor neuron degeneration
options.rationale: Wrong: Respiratory failure is typical in ALS due to motor neuron loss; UMN/LMN signs and ALS pathology would be needed to justify this cause.

options.text: Sudden cardiac arrhythmia from hypertrophic cardiomyopathy
options.rationale: Wrong: Hypertrophic cardiomyopathy is associated with Friedreich’s ataxia and can cause sudden death; cardiac imaging showing HCM would be necessary to support this.

question: Which lecture point explains why some spinocerebellar ataxias show neuronal intranuclear inclusions similar to Huntington disease?
cited_learning_objective: Explain the relationship of Friedreich’s ataxia to other spinocerebellar ataxias

options.text: Several SCAs are caused by CAG repeat polyglutamine expansions
options.rationale: Correct: The lecture notes many SCAs are due to CAG repeat expansions encoding polyglutamine tracts, producing neuronal intranuclear inclusions similar to Huntington disease.

options.text: All SCAs are autosomal recessive like Friedreich’s ataxia
options.rationale: Wrong: Most SCAs are autosomal dominant; Friedreich’s ataxia is an exception being autosomal recessive, so this statement is incorrect.

options.text: SCAs result from mitochondrial frataxin deficiency always
options.rationale: Wrong: Frataxin deficiency causes Friedreich’s ataxia specifically, not all SCAs; GAA frataxin expansions would be required to support that mechanism.

options.text: SCAs are caused by alpha-synuclein oligodendroglial inclusions
options.rationale: Wrong: α-synuclein oligodendroglial inclusions characterize MSA, not SCAs; oligodendroglial pathology would be needed to support MSA instead.

options.text: SCAs are due to autoimmune cerebellar Purkinje cell loss
options.rationale: Wrong: Autoimmune cerebellar degeneration is a separate entity; genetic repeat expansions rather than autoimmune mechanisms explain many SCAs per the lecture.

question: Which clinical sign would most strongly argue against a diagnosis of ALS based on the lecture descriptions?
cited_learning_objective: Summarize the clinical features, pathogenesis, and morphological characteristics of amyotrophic lateral sclerosis

options.text: Prominent sensory loss with numbness and paresthesias
options.rationale: Correct: The lecture emphasizes ALS lacks sensory deficits; prominent sensory loss would argue against ALS and suggest peripheral neuropathy or other disorders.

options.text: Progressive dysarthria with tongue atrophy and fasciculations
options.rationale: Wrong: These are classic LMN bulbar signs seen in ALS and would support rather than argue against the diagnosis.

options.text: Spastic limb weakness with hyperreflexia and clonus
options.rationale: Wrong: These UMN signs are consistent with ALS when combined with LMN features; their presence supports ALS rather than excludes it.

options.text: Rapidly progressive dementia with visual hallucinations
options.rationale: Wrong: Rapid dementia and hallucinations suggest prion disease or Lewy body dementia, but alone do not definitively exclude ALS unless motor neuron signs are absent.

options.text: Orthostatic hypotension with urinary incontinence
options.rationale: Wrong: Autonomic failure with orthostatic hypotension and incontinence points toward MSA rather than ALS; autonomic testing would clarify the diagnosis.

question: Which lecture-described therapeutic approach is specifically mentioned as available for a genetic motor neuron disease of childhood?
cited_learning_objective: Summarize the clinical features, pathogenesis, and morphological characteristics of amyotrophic lateral sclerosis

options.text: Gene therapy (Zolgensma) for spinal muscular atrophy
options.rationale: Correct: The lecture mentions gene therapy (Zolgensma) as a treatment for SMA caused by SMN1 loss-of-function mutations.

options.text: Chelation therapy for frataxin GAA repeat disease
options.rationale: Wrong: Chelation treats Wilson disease copper overload, not frataxin GAA repeat Friedreich’s ataxia; copper studies would be needed to justify chelation.

options.text: Dopamine agonists curing Parkinson disease permanently
options.rationale: Wrong: Dopamine replacement improves symptoms but does not cure PD; evidence of neuronal regeneration would be required to claim a cure.

options.text: Antisense oligonucleotides reversing Huntington disease
options.rationale: Wrong: While experimental therapies exist, the lecture does not state antisense oligonucleotides reverse HD; demonstration of effective reversal would be necessary to support this.

options.text: Thiamine replacement reversing Wernicke encephalopathy always
options.rationale: Wrong: Thiamine can improve Wernicke encephalopathy but not always reverse established damage; early treatment improves outcomes but is not guaranteed to fully reverse pathology.

question: A patient with suspected parkinsonism has minimal improvement with levodopa and early cognitive decline within months. Which diagnosis from the lecture is most consistent with these features?
cited_learning_objective: Understand the pathogenesis of Parkinson’s disease and “atypical” Parkinson’s disease

options.text: Dementia with Lewy bodies presenting early cognitive decline
options.rationale: Correct: The lecture distinguishes dementia with Lewy bodies when cognitive and motor symptoms onset are within one year, often with poor levodopa response.

options.text: Idiopathic Parkinson disease with late dementia onset
options.rationale: Wrong: Idiopathic PD-associated dementia occurs when cognitive symptoms begin more than one year after motor onset; earlier cognitive onset would argue against this.

options.text: Progressive supranuclear palsy with vertical gaze palsy
options.rationale: Wrong: PSP features gaze palsy and early falls; absence of gaze palsy and presence of early cognitive decline within months favors Lewy body dementia.

options.text: Multiple system atrophy with predominant autonomic failure
options.rationale: Wrong: MSA presents with autonomic dysfunction and oligodendroglial inclusions; prominent autonomic signs would be needed to support MSA.

options.text: Wilson disease with hepatic failure causing neuropsychiatric signs
options.rationale: Wrong: Wilson disease can cause neuropsychiatric symptoms but typically has hepatic involvement and abnormal copper studies; those findings would be required to diagnose WD.

question: Which lecture point explains why some Parkinsonian syndromes are minimally responsive to L-DOPA?
cited_learning_objective: Understand the pathogenesis of Parkinson’s disease and “atypical” Parkinson’s disease

options.text: Pathology involves non-dopaminergic systems or nonneuronal inclusions
options.rationale: Correct: Atypical parkinsonian syndromes involve additional circuits (e.g., tauopathies, oligodendroglial inclusions) so dopamine replacement is often minimally effective as noted in the lecture.

options.text: Levodopa is ineffective due to frataxin deficiency
options.rationale: Wrong: Frataxin deficiency causes Friedreich’s ataxia and is unrelated to levodopa responsiveness; frataxin testing would be needed to implicate FA.

options.text: Levodopa cannot cross the blood brain barrier
options.rationale: Wrong: Levodopa does cross the BBB and is effective in idiopathic PD; failure to respond is due to pathology beyond dopaminergic neuron loss rather than BBB impermeability.

options.text: Dopamine antagonists permanently block levodopa action
options.rationale: Wrong: Dopamine antagonists can induce parkinsonism but do not permanently block levodopa; discontinuation of antagonists often restores levodopa responsiveness.

options.text: SMN1 mutations prevent dopamine synthesis in striatum
options.rationale: Wrong: SMN1 mutations cause SMA via spliceosome defects, not striatal dopamine synthesis impairment; SMN1 mutation evidence would be required to support this.

question: Which lecture-described neuropathologic hallmark differentiates multiple system atrophy from Parkinson disease at the cellular level?
cited_learning_objective: Understand the pathogenesis of Parkinson’s disease and “atypical” Parkinson’s disease

options.text: Alpha-synuclein inclusions in oligodendrocytes in MSA
options.rationale: Correct: The lecture specifies MSA has α-synuclein inclusions in oligodendrocytes, whereas PD has neuronal Lewy bodies.

options.text: Neuronal Lewy bodies composed of alpha-synuclein
options.rationale: Wrong: Neuronal Lewy bodies are characteristic of PD; finding neuronal rather than oligodendroglial inclusions would indicate PD/Lewy body disease.

options.text: Ubiquitinated intranuclear huntingtin inclusions
options.rationale: Wrong: Ubiquitinated huntingtin inclusions are seen in Huntington disease; CAG expansion evidence would be needed to support HD pathology.

options.text: Tau positive inclusions in brainstem and cortex
options.rationale: Wrong: Tau inclusions are typical of PSP and corticobasal degeneration; tau immunoreactivity and clinical gaze palsy or apraxia would favor those diagnoses.

options.text: TDP-43 cytoplasmic inclusions in motor neurons
options.rationale: Wrong: TDP-43 inclusions are linked to ALS/FTD; motor neuron degeneration and UMN/LMN signs would be required to support TDP-43 pathology.

question: Which lecture point about Huntington disease explains why age of onset varies with repeat length but disease course does not?
cited_learning_objective: Explain the genetics of Huntington disease

options.text: Repeat length correlates with age of onset not symptom severity
options.rationale: Correct: The lecture explicitly states CAG repeat length correlates with age of onset but not with severity of initial symptoms or overall course.

options.text: Repeat length determines both onset age and disease duration
options.rationale: Wrong: The lecture contradicts this; repeat length affects onset age but not disease course, so this statement would be correct only if evidence linked length to duration.

options.text: Repeat expansions only occur in maternal transmission
options.rationale: Wrong: The lecture notes expansions tend to occur in the male line during spermatogenesis; maternal-only expansion would be inconsistent with the lecture.

options.text: CAG repeats encode polyalanine tracts causing toxicity
options.rationale: Wrong: CAG encodes glutamine producing polyglutamine tracts; polyalanine encoding would be incorrect and not match HD molecular pathology.

options.text: Huntingtin loss of function causes immediate neuronal death
options.rationale: Wrong: The lecture states the function of huntingtin and mechanisms of toxicity are unknown; immediate loss-of-function causing instant neuronal death is not described.

question: Which lecture-described vitamin deficiency causes hemorrhagic necrosis in mammillary bodies and ophthalmoplegia?
cited_learning_objective: Provide examples of acquired metabolic and toxic agent-induced diseases relevant to disease in the CNS

options.text: Thiamine deficiency causing Wernicke encephalopathy
options.rationale: Correct: The lecture states thiamine (B1) deficiency causes Wernicke encephalopathy with hemorrhage and necrosis in mammillary bodies and ophthalmoplegia.

options.text: Vitamin B12 deficiency causing subacute combined degeneration
options.rationale: Wrong: B12 deficiency causes subacute combined degeneration of the spinal cord with dorsal column involvement, not mammillary body hemorrhage.

options.text: Vitamin E deficiency causing peripheral neuropathy
options.rationale: Wrong: Vitamin E deficiency can cause neuropathy but is not described in the lecture as causing mammillary body hemorrhage or ophthalmoplegia.

options.text: Niacin deficiency causing pellagra with dermatitis
options.rationale: Wrong: Niacin deficiency causes pellagra (dermatitis, diarrhea, dementia), not mammillary body hemorrhage; dermatologic and GI signs would be expected.

options.text: Folate deficiency causing megaloblastic anemia only
options.rationale: Wrong: Folate deficiency causes megaloblastic anemia and neural tube defects but is not linked to mammillary body hemorrhage and ophthalmoplegia described in Wernicke encephalopathy.
